text: So this, it was a seventy-five-year-old man who presented with an anterior mediastinal mass. A needle biopsy confirmed an invasive thymoma, and as it was quite large, the patient underwent induction chemotherapy with CAP regimen with very little change in size of the mass.
Given the potential for invasion of the lung, we anticipated requiring to perform possibly a wedge resection of the lung. in addition.
The patient is positioned supine with the right arm resting by the side, and a bump is placed under the right chest to optimize surgical access.

Three incisions are made for the thymectomy: a camera port in the fifth intercostal space, a right-handed port in the seventh intercostal space, and a left-handed port in the third intercostal space. The camera is positioned below the plane of the third and seventh intercostal space ports.
The surgeon places a subxiphoid incision and inserts a twelve millimeter port for the fourth arm of the robot, preparing to use the robotic stapler for a potential wedge resection.
The camera is positioned intrapleurally, and the chest cavity is insufflated, revealing the approximately six and a half centimeter thymoma within the anterior mediastinum.

Under direct vision, additional ports are placed, including the creation of a subxiphoid port. The surgeon incises the fascia and uses blunt dissection to extend the plane towards the ten or eleven o'clock position, with a camera in the chest guiding the trocar placement.
A twelve millimeter trocar is placed, followed by the insertion of a trocar into the third interspace under thoracoscopic visualization.
The surgeon manually holds the robotic camera while preparing to dock the robot using the XI platform after placing the trocars.
The robotic arms are positioned parallel to the line of the trocars, with the camera being attached first, followed by the other robotic instruments.
The surgeon adjusts the robotic arms for optimal clearance between the third and fifth interspace ports, navigating the limited space within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to retract the mass in the right middle lobe, which is adherent to surrounding tissue.
in the anterior twelve millimeter port. Now, it's really important when you do any thymic work, but especially with thymomas, even if they're well encapsulated, to perform a no-touch technique.

The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector in the right hand through the seventh interspace port and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand through the third interspace port, carefully manipulating the thymoma to avoid violating the capsule and seeding the pleural cavity.

The surgeon performs a wedge resection of a small piece of the right middle lobe of the lung, which is adherent to the tumor mass, leaving it attached to the tumor to avoid taking down adhesions that could lead to local recurrence.

The surgeon uses a Blue Load, 45-millimeter robotic stapler to perform tissue manipulation, ensuring the mass is not touched during the procedure. The stapler is controlled entirely from the surgical console.
This has been speeded up slightly.
So in retrospect, it may have been feasible to just take those adhesions down. They don't look particularly severe. Nonetheless, this is probably safer.
The surgeon uses a dental roll with a radiopaque ribbon threaded through its center to gently retract the mass, minimizing the risk of fracturing the capsule.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to carefully separate the phrenic nerve from the surrounding tissue, sweeping it posteriorly away from the mass. The nerve appears uninvolved with the mass, as indicated by preoperative imaging.
The trainee uses bipolar dissectors to cautiously dissect tissue, applying energy while gradually closing the tips of the jaws to ensure effective coagulation.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully enter the pericardium due to the large size and adherence of the mass, opting to excise a portion of the pericardium along with the thymus tissue.
The surgeon identifies a small IIIa lymph node adjacent to the mass and prepares to remove it along with the specimen.

So in this case we're using a thirty degree scope which offers some benefit I think in being able to look down on tissue but also being able to look up either at the chest wall or underneath the tumor if you're dissecting underneath it. But it's not mandatory, you can do this perfectly well with a zero degree scope as well.

The surgeon visualizes the innominate vein and the right internal mammary vein using a thirty-degree scope. A surgical sponge is temporarily positioned but reconsidered due to potential difficulty in retrieval if it falls into the pericardium. The mediastinal fatty tissue is observed, noting that complete removal is not necessary due to the absence of myasthenia gravis symptoms.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree up view to separate the pleura from the chest wall, ensuring adequate margins on the specimen. The anterior mediastinal fat is divided below the tumor, while remaining anterior to the heart to avoid phrenic nerve injury. As the dissection progresses cephalad, the course of the contralateral phrenic nerve is assessed.
The surgeon carefully removes a surgical sponge, referred to as a 'peanut,' from the chest cavity to prevent it from being misplaced in an undesirable location.

The surgeon opens the pleura in the upper portion of the anterior mediastinum, revealing the contralateral lung. The incision is extended from the upper mediastinum to the diaphragm, allowing visualization of the opposite phrenic nerve and facilitating specimen removal by placing the specimen bag into the opposite chest.
The surgeon searches for the right thymic horn, carefully cauterizing small arterial branches within the thymic tissue to prevent bleeding and retraction.

The surgeon uses a robotic fourth arm to retract and manipulate the bulky thymic tumor, facilitating its removal without the need for a bedside assistant. The absence of an assist port is noted, emphasizing the reliance on robotic assistance for retraction.

The surgeon identifies the left thymic tissue and proceeds to divide small tributaries off the innominate vein. Smaller tributaries are cauterized, while larger ones are tied to ensure proper division.
The surgeon uses hemlock clips or five millimeter clips, placed by an assistant, to secure vessels or tissue during the thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon removes the tip-up grasper through the twelve millimeter port, while the scrub tech passes a two-oh silk ligature into the surgical field. The tip-up grasper is reinserted to provide cephalad retraction on the thymic tissue, enhancing visualization of the thymic vein without touching the mass itself.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree downward view to visually assess the course of the phrenic nerve, ensuring that dissection is performed away from the nerve, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
Again, measure twice, cut once type of thing.
The surgeon carefully holds onto the thymic masses with graspers to prevent them from slipping into deeper portions of the chest during release, ensuring they remain securely in view.

The surgeon removes the retraction instrument and the twelve millimeter port, then enlarges the subxiphoid incision to extract the thymic mass through the subxiphoid port. A bag is placed into the left chest to facilitate the mass's entry without direct pressure on the tumor, avoiding capsular tear and potential pleural space seeding.

The surgeon extracts the specimen by slightly enlarging the subxiphoid incision, revealing the mass with two thymic horns and the pericardium. A 24 French chest tube is inserted before closing the fascia.
